elements: A sequence of 5 black wireframe camera frustums spaced evenly along their forward optical axis
Thick red arrows pointing along the translation direction, connecting consecutive camera projection centers (vertices)
An active, highlighted camera model that can slide smoothly between the 5 positions
Reference grid floor to ground the spatial translation
Optical axis line (Z-axis) showing the path of translation
Faint text labels indicating Step 1 through Step 5 next to each camera position

interactions.label: Camera Position Step
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates the progression of the camera moving forward along its optical axis, transitioning from the first exposure state to the fifth

interactions.label: Show all 5 frustums
interactions.teaches: Toggles the visualization of the entire multi-exposure sequence to show the overall path of motion versus focusing on a single moving camera

interactions.label: Animate camera motion
interactions.teaches: Toggles a continuous, smooth forward translation cycle of the camera along the Z-axis path

demo_steps.title: Camera Forward Translation Setup
demo_steps.narration: This visual model represents a camera moving straight forward along its optical axis. We see five consecutive camera frustums. The black wireframes show the camera's field of view at discrete intervals, and the thick red arrows indicate the direction of forward translation.

demo_steps.title: Stepping Through the Sequence
demo_steps.narration: As we scrub the camera position forward, notice how the camera's viewpoint moves progressively along the optical axis (Z-axis). This represents pure translation where lateral velocities V_X and V_Y are zero, leaving only V_Z active.

demo_steps.title: Continuous Forward Flow
demo_steps.narration: With continuous animation active, observe the smooth translation. This physical displacement is what generates the optical flow field: as the camera advances, static scene points appear to expand outward from the camera's heading direction—the Focus of Expansion.

plan.camera_suggestion: Elevated 3/4 perspective view, looking down at the camera track from the side to clearly visualize the sequential displacement of the five camera bodies and their forward-pointing red arrows.
plan.notes: Render the camera frustums as clean, thick black outlines. Use simple conical or box-arrow geometries for the red transition arrows between pinhole centers. When 'showAllSteps' is true, set the opacity of inactive frustums to 0.4, while keeping the active step fully opaque.

elements: 3D camera rig consisting of a wireframe frustum, focal point (pinhole), and a virtual image plane
A target wall (planar surface) with a grid pattern perpendicular to the camera axis
A set of 3D feature points scattered in space between the camera and the wall to show depth variation
Visual representation of the optical axis (Z-axis) extending from the camera to the wall
A secondary 2D 'Camera View' panel overlay showing the projection of the 3D points onto the image plane
Optical flow vector arrows drawn on the 2D camera view representing the instantaneous motion field (dx/dt, dy/dt)
A bright red marker representing the 'Focus of Expansion' (FOE) on both the 3D image plane and 2D overlay
An animated camera path line showing the trajectory of forward translation

interactions.label: Camera Depth (Z)
interactions.teaches: Moving the camera closer to the wall decreases Time-to-Contact, causing the optical flow vectors to grow exponentially as 1/Z

interactions.label: Forward Velocity (V_z)
interactions.teaches: Higher forward velocity directly scales the magnitude of the outward flow vectors, keeping the expansion center identical but increasing the rate of scaling

interactions.label: Lateral Velocity (V_x)
interactions.teaches: Adding lateral motion shifts the Focus of Expansion (FOE) away from the center of the screen, tracking the precise direction of the camera's 3D motion vector

demo_steps.title: The Focus of Expansion
demo_steps.narration: When a camera moves straight forward (V_x = 0, V_z = 1.5), all optical flow vectors radiate outward from a single stationary point on the image plane called the Focus of Expansion (FOE). Notice that points closer to the center move very slowly, while points in the periphery expand rapidly outward.

demo_steps.title: The Impact of Velocity
demo_steps.narration: Double the forward velocity to V_z = 3.0. Observe how all the flow vectors immediately double in length. The formula dx/dt = (V_z / Z) * x shows a linear relationship: driving faster directly intensifies the rate of expansion across the entire visual field.

demo_steps.title: Approaching the Wall (1/Z Scale)
demo_steps.narration: Now let's drive the camera closer to the wall, reducing depth Z to 3.5. As Time-to-Contact (Z / V_z) decreases, the optical flow vectors explode in size! This dramatic enlargement shows the powerful inverse-depth (1/Z) scaling effect — closer features appear to move much faster than distant ones.

demo_steps.title: Shifting the Trajectory
demo_steps.narration: Introduce a lateral velocity offset (V_x = -1.0). The Focus of Expansion (marked in red) shifts horizontally to the left. The camera is now heading toward a point on the left side of the wall, and the entire flow field instantly realigns to expand outward from this new heading point.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Split viewport: The main 3D canvas should show a perspective perspective-view of the camera rig approaching the wall from a 3/4 high-angle viewpoint to clearly show camera translation. A smaller, locked 2D overlay in the top-right corner displays the projected flow field from the camera's actual point-of-view.
interactionPlan.notes: Represent the optical flow vectors as colored lines (e.g., cyan/blue gradient) on the 2D HUD. The length of each vector at screen coordinate (x, y) should be calculated dynamically as dx = (V_x * f - x * V_z) / Z and dy = (-y * V_z) / Z, where f is the focal length. The FOE coordinates on screen are (f * V_x / V_z, f * V_y / V_z). Render dynamic numerical readouts of 'Time-to-Contact' (Z / V_z) next to the camera model.

attempts.html:
The Focus of Expansion
All optical flow vectors radiate from the Focus of Expansion.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The source figure shows a sequential progression of five camera frustums from left to right connected by red arrows, whereas the generated figure shows a single camera moving toward a grid wall.
The generated scene contains a complex grid-patterned target wall, floating feature points, and a 2D camera view overlay HUD that do not exist in the source image.
The source image contains simple red directional arrows between the cameras, which are missing in the generated layout.
The generated code includes text labels and UI sliders for velocity and depth that are completely absent in the original figure.
evaluation.notes: The generated figure is a highly complex interactive optical flow simulator, which completely misunderstands and replaces the simple 5-camera sequence shown in the source figure.
action_items: Completely redesign the scene to display a sequence of five camera frustums from left to right as depicted in the original image.
Remove all complex elements such as the target wall, floating dots, and the top-right HUD camera view.
Render simple thick red arrows pointing to the right between the consecutive black pinhole camera vertices.
Provide simple controls to slide/step through the 5 camera positions or toggle a moving virtual camera along this multi-step path.

feedback.rationale: The planner completely misunderstood the source concept (generating an optical flow simulator instead of a 5-camera sequence), requiring a total redesign of the interaction plan and scene decomposition.
action_items: Completely redesign the scene to display a sequence of five camera frustums from left to right as depicted in the original image.
Remove all complex elements such as the target wall, floating dots, and the top-right HUD camera view.
Render simple thick red arrows pointing to the right between the consecutive black pinhole camera vertices.
Provide simple controls to slide/step through the 5 camera positions or toggle a moving virtual camera along this multi-step path.
actionItems: Completely redesign the scene to display a sequence of five camera frustums from left to right as depicted in the original image.
Remove all complex elements such as the target wall, floating dots, and the top-right HUD camera view.
Render simple thick red arrows pointing to the right between the consecutive black pinhole camera vertices.
Provide simple controls to slide/step through the 5 camera positions or toggle a moving virtual camera along this multi-step path.

attempts.html:
Five-camera motion sequence
Red arrows indicate translation through successive camera positions.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The generated image contains several text labels ('camera 1', 'motion / time direction', etc.) that are completely absent in the original figure.
The camera models have an interior vertical gray line that differs in tone from the outer black lines of the camera wireframes.
The active frame has a red highlight halo, which does not exist in the original textbook figure.
evaluation.notes: The simulation is highly interactive and functional, but it deviates significantly from the original figure by adding multiple text labels and highlight effects that are not in the source image.
action_items: Disable or remove the default-on labels ('camera 1-5', 'motion / time direction', 'five equal camera positions') to match the textbook's clean and unlabeled presentation.
Remove the red circular highlight/halo around the active camera when 'Show all 5 cameras' is enabled to maintain high visual faithfulness.
Ensure all line segments of the camera frustum wireframes are drawn with a consistent solid black material, matching the original illustration's style.
Adjust spacing between cameras and red arrows so that the arrows cleanly bridge the gaps without intersecting the camera frustum bodies.

feedback.rationale: The core concept and interactivity are highly rated, but the execution needs concrete implementation fixes to remove hallucinated labels and styling highlights, which is best handled by refining the generation.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The internal lines of the camera wireframes have slightly different gray/black shading values compared to the source.
The red arrow shafts and heads are slightly bulkier than the thin, sleek arrows in the original drawing.
evaluation.notes: Excellent replication of the 2D sequence into an interactive 3D space with helpful viewing options and animation.
action_items: Fine-tune the wireframe cylinder thicknesses to match the delicate lines of the source figure.
Make the red arrows slightly thinner and more elongated to improve visual faithfulness.
Add minor hover or click effects on individual camera nodes to show details about each sequence step.

feedback.rationale: Scores are consistently high at 4/5, with no critical failure modes identified and only minor visual polishing action items, indicating the figure is of sufficient quality to pass.
